title: Bottled Water Brands To AVOID at All Costs (And 2 That Won’t Poison You)

chunks.title: Introduction and Purpose
chunks.summary: The video introduces the analysis of eight popular bottled waters, revealing unexpected results regarding heavy metals, radioactive elements, and microplastics. The speaker shares the motivation behind independent testing and the importance of awareness for consumers.
chunks.transcript: This water may look clean. To be honest, it probably tastes fine. But when I sent eight brands of popular bottled waters, both glass and plastic, to an independent laboratory, the results change how I think about every glass of water that I drink. In this video, I'm going to share with you the results of this independent analysis and rank all eight of these bottled waters from best to worst. We're going to talk about heavy metal levels, things like lead, mercury, cadmium, arsenic. We're going to talk about radioactive elements with like uranium, which are actually in a couple of these bottled waters. We're going to talk about microplastics, which are way worse than you think. And I bet you actually bought the worst water in [music] this group within the last week. So, let's get into it. I drink a lot of bottled water when I'm traveling. When I'm at home, I'm drinking reverse osmosis water that's remineralized. But when I'm traveling, I'm drinking some of these waters. I'll tell you which one I drink at the very end of this video.

chunks.title: Testing Methodology
chunks.summary: The speaker explains the testing process conducted in partnership with the Animalbased Nutrition Research Foundation, detailing the specific contaminants tested. A focus is placed on the potential accumulation of harmful substances over time due to regular consumption of bottled water.
chunks.transcript: There's a lot of people analyzing waters and sharing their results. Momation, Oasis, Tap Score. They're giving number grades out of a 100. Some waters 55 out of 100. Other waters are 45 out of 100. But here's what bother me. None of these platforms are actually sharing all of their results. None of them is being completely transparent and showing certificates of analysis. I appreciate what they're doing. I love that they're informing us as consumers, but nobody is really sharing all of the hard data. So, I wanted to do something different. I actually wanted to test these on our own. I wanted to test them myself. So, I partnered with the Animalbased Nutrition Research Foundation. That's a nonprofit that I started. And we sent these eight bottled waters to an independent lab. And I will show you a link at the end of this video to the actual certificates of analysis at the ABNRF website. We will publish a full blog where you can actually click on the full results of all of these. We tested them for 32 minerals, heavy metals, radioactive elements, and we tested them for microplastics. And in this video, I'm going to break it all down for you guys. So, first you're probably asking, why does this even matter? The amounts of heavy metals and contaminants in these might seem low when I tell you the actual number amounts, but think about how much water you drink on a daily basis. Think about how much water you drink every week. Think about how much water you drink every year or over the course of your life. The average American drinks 365 L of bottled water from sources like this per year. Over the course of your life, that's over 10,000 plus lers of bottled water. That's a big deal. This is going to add up. Even if one of these waters or multiple of these waters contains trace amounts or small amounts of contaminants that are actually harmful for humans, if you're always drinking the same thing, this could accumulate in your body over time. The other thing to think about is that when you're drinking liters of water, you're drinking kilograms of a substance. A liter is [music] a kilogram, right? That's 1,000 milliliters. When you're thinking about food, there are very few foods that you are going to eat kilogram quantities of on a daily basis. But most of us drink a kilogram of water every single day of our lives. So water, this may sound obvious, but it's true, is probably the single biggest thing that we consume. It's really important to get this right and to know what's in your water. You might eat 200 g of rice, but you're drinking thousands of grams of water per day. And here's the last piece to really consider. What your water comes in matters. We've got five glass bottles and three plastic bottles. You guys probably have a sense of this, but when you're drinking out of a plastic bottle, that's going to result in a very different composition of the water with regard to things like microplastics and nanoplastics than a glass bottle. There recently was a study comparing [music] glass and plastic bottled waters for microplastics. And I've got some criticisms of that study. I'm going to get to it at the end of this video when I talk specifically about microplastics.

chunks.title: Overview of Bottled Waters Tested
chunks.summary: Nine bottled waters, both glass and plastic, are introduced along with a discussion of their popularity. The speaker mentions the purpose of selecting specific brands for testing based on consumer preference and availability.
chunks.transcript: But know this, what your water comes in matters in a huge way. So with all that said, let's get into the actual results and break this down water by water. So we tested these nine bottles actually of bottled water for heavy metals and other contaminants. What did we test for? We tested for lead, a heavy metal linked to neurodedevelopmental issues in humans. Very, very problematic for humans. We tested for mercury linked to kidney issues, cancers, [music] hormonal disruption. You're probably thinking about mercury with regard to fish, but it can also be in your water. We tested for arsenic, a known carcinogen. There's a lot of arsenic in rice, but some of these waters have been implicated for containing arsenic. We tested for cadmium, a heavy metal also linked to cancers and kidney issues, which is quite high in shellfish and bottom feeders. And we tested for it in these waters. We tested for aluminum, like your aluminum foil. Excess amounts of aluminum in the human diet have been linked to increased incidence of dementia like Alzheimer's dementia and cognitive issues. You don't want excess aluminum in your body. We tested for titanium and we tested for uranium, a radioactive element which occurs naturally in the earth, but excess amounts of which are definitely linked to cancers in humans. So, what do we have here? We've got vos in glass. We've got mountain valley in glass. This one's come under some scrutiny recently, but we'll talk about it. We've got Icelandic water in glass. We've got aquapana. Got to give the Italians a shout out in glass. And we've got recently Instagram famous Saratoga in glass right here. Maybe in this video at some point I'll actually break this and it'll make a good clip. In terms of plastic bottled waters, we've got Smart Water in plastic, Fiji in plastic, Evian in plastic, and this is Deer Park water in plastic. When I was growing up, we used to joke that Evian was naive, spelled backwards. Doesn't quite work that way. We'll find out if Evian is really worth your money in at the end of this video. For this video, we tried to select the most popular waters that you guys were drinking. I surveyed you guys and I said, "What do you guys want me to test? If there are other bottled waters you want us to test in connection with the ABNRF, put them in the comments and I will test those in the future. But these are the ones that I mostly see in grocery stores and airports. And so I wanted to test the most valuable, most commonly occurring waters so that you guys know what to buy when you're reaching for a bottle of bottled water in the store. Okay, so let's get into ranking these waters. To do this in the

chunks.title: Ranking of Bottled Waters: Mid-Tier
chunks.summary: The speaker begins ranking the bottled waters from best to worst, discussing Aquapana, Saratoga, and Mountain Valley. Key findings include levels of contaminants and issues regarding the source and quality of the water.
chunks.transcript: best way, I'm going to put them all over here and I'm going to reorder them one at a time in a unique fashion. So, let's start with Aquapana. This water is in glass. This water comes in at number four. So, this is four out of nine. It's kind of in the middle. Again, shout out to the Italians. Saladino. Got to give a shout out. Why is this only number four? When we tested Aquapana, it didn't have any detectable levels of heavy metals. That's good, positive. But it did have more uranium than other waters in the test. There were two bottled waters in the test that had notable levels of uranium radioactive element. Again, the amount of uranium in Aquapana is pretty low. All things considered, it's.9 parts per billion. You'd have to drink a lot of it to be a problem. A little bit every once in a while is not a big deal. Your kidneys can get rid of it. But in the grand scheme of things, there were other bottled waters that had no uranium and no heavy metals and no contaminants. So, I have to rank Aquapana number four. Still good, but there are [music] better waters. >> The plastic rankings, we don't have a smart water. >> Well, we'll just take it out. >> Yeah, just take it out. >> Next, we're going to rank Saratoga. This is the darling of the internet. Ashton Hall made this one famous. It's got a beautiful blue bottle. It says it's natural spring water, but it actually comes in below Aquapana at number five. What are my issues with Saratoga? In independent tests, there have been multiple reports showing that certain batches of Saratoga have brs of total trihalomethanes. These are contaminants related to chlorine in the water. They shouldn't be in a spring water. This suggests, and a lot of people are talking about this, that Saratoga, because it went viral, because somebody broke it on the ground and because it's in a pretty blue bottle, they may actually be mixing their spring water with a municipal source. That downgrades it significantly. If you're paying for spring water, you want spring water. When we tested for heavy metals, there actually were no detectable heavy metals in the Saratoga. So, that's a positive. No uranium in the Saratoga, but based on the multiple reports of potential mixing with municipal sources, the bromeforms, and the total trialmethane, this guy's got to be number five. There are multiple better waters out there. Next, let's rank Mountain Valley. Mountain Valley comes in at number three. It doesn't get the top two spots. It gets the bronze metal here. It's got a beautiful green bottle and it is supposed to be spring water. When we tested Mountain Valley for contaminants, it was very clean. It did not have any detectable heavy metals. There's a story behind this, though. There's a pending class action lawsuit against Mountain Valley for two things. There's a test from 2019 that found arsenic at 1.1 parts per billion in Mountain Valley, and there's a test from 2025 that found.16 parts per billion of arsenic in Mountain Valley. Our testing went down to 0.15 and we did not see any arsenic in Mountain Valley. So it brings up the question of variability between batches. The first thing to understand is that 0.15 parts per billion of arsenic is very low. If you're eating rice, rice is often 50 to 100 or 150 parts per billion up to 400 parts per billion of arsenic. I've done other videos on rice. I'm not going to go into rice in detail in this video. Suffice it to say that if you're eating brown rice, that's even higher than white rice and it's hundreds if not thousands of times more arsenic than Mountain Valley [music] water. So, I don't really worry about Mountain Valley water with the arsenic. The variability makes me think they have some quality issues. Again, just like Saratoga, Mountain Valley has become quite popular and they might be sourcing from different places. We will see. There have been subsequent tests which did not detect arsenic like ours in Mountain Valley. But like Saratoga, certain tests have also detected brforms. Again, these are breakdown products of chlorine treatment of the water, suggesting that some batches of Mountain Valley might be mixed with municipal sources. So, that's why Mountain Valley ends up at number three with continued concerns over water quality, water mixing, but no levels of heavy metals or uranium detected in our tests. So, which one gets the silver medal? Silver medal goes to Voss. Should

chunks.title: Ranking of Bottled Waters: Top Choices
chunks.summary: The second half of the ranking focuses on Voss and Icelandic water, touted as the best bottled water available. Discussion revolves around the absence of contaminants and the overall quality of these waters compared to others.
chunks.transcript: we play the Olympic anthem here? What do we like about Voss? Glass bottle. When we [music] tested, no detectable levels of lead, mercury, cadmium or arsenic. chunks.title: Ranking of Bottled Waters: Bottom Tier
chunks.summary: The speaker reveals the bottom-tier rankings, highlighting Deer Park, Fiji, and Evian as disappointing options based on contamination levels, including BPA and heavy metals. Emphasis is placed on the negative health implications of consuming these waters.
chunks.transcript: now and we'll see the worst bottled waters. So, coming in at number six, we've got Deer Park Water. What do we like about this? Deer Park Water had no heavy metals in the testing, but all of the bottles in plastic come with contamination that heavy metal testing cannot actually see. This is the microplastics, the nanoplastics conversation. What's important about this? There recently was a controversial study released that actually claimed that glass bottled waters had higher levels of microplastics than plastic bottled water. This study is bunk. It's only looking at large microplastic fragments. and it was showing levels four to six microplastics per liter. That's big particles. We're talking about particles of plastic that are greater than one micrometer, one micrometer. So that's a micrometer. The real problem with plastic bottle water are much smaller particles. You need a very sophisticated way of testing to do this and it's not a lot of labs that can actually test for nanoplastics. When you're thinking about nanoplastic levels, this is mostly at the research level, university research lab. And there are multiple studies done at this very granular level that have shown up to 250,000 nanoplastics. These are particles of plastic 50 to 500 nanometers. So much smaller. 1,000 nanometers is one micrometer. Right? So 50 nanometers is 20 times smaller than a micrometer size microplastic. And if you look for very small particles of plastic, you see that plastic bottled waters are teeming with this. Glass bottled waters don't have this problem. So don't be swayed into thinking that plastic bottled water has less microplastics than glass bottled water because the testing they did was poor and it was only looking at big particles. We know very clearly that plastic bottled water is just full of microplastics. And think about this. This is sitting on a shelf for 6 months, 8 months in heat, in sunlight or blue lights in the grocery store. This bottle is going to break down and go into this water and that is going to create a water that is teeming with microplastics. I've done other content about what the biggest sources of microplastics are for humans. Bottled water is a huge one. Soup cans which are filled hot. Paper cups at Starbucks that you think are paper are actually lined with plastic. Those are all major contaminant sources for microplastics in humans which are a big problem because they can also be endocrine disruptors. They can carry things that are endocrine disruptors like PFAS, paraphlloro substances. They can carry BPA and BPS like a Trojan horse. They can ride on these microplastics into your body. Microplastics have been found in every single tissue in the human body, the testicles, the brain. We are teeming with microplastics. We probably consume grams of them per year and this is a big source. So plastic bottled water never beats glass bottled water in my opinion. The good thing about the deer park if you're in a pinch is that the metals were not detected in the deer park and there was no uranium. Number seven is Fiji water. The problem with Fiji is that it contained arsenic at 0.9 parts per billion and it was the only water of all the waters tested that contained forever chemicals. These are PIFAs paraphlloro alkal substances. They accumulate in your body. They're endocrine disruptors. They're called forever chemicals because they're persistent in the environment and it's pretty hard for your body to get rid of them. So if you're drinking a lot of Fiji water, and I used to like this water, it's colorful. It has good branding. Has a colorful flower on it that reminds me of Costa Rica. It says it's artisian water. You can definitely accumulate arsenic from that and arsenic again is linked to cancers. So Fiji was a pretty big disappointment. But the biggest disappointment in the whole test was Evian. So maybe Evian spelled backward is naive because even though this bottled water is very popular and over 6 million bottles of this are sold every single day, Evian had a lot of problems. Evian had the highest amount of uranium in the test at 1.9 parts per billion. It also contained over 400 parts per billion aluminum. Again, aluminum is that metal that's linked to dementia and high concentrations in the human body. So, I don't want extra aluminum in my water. I also don't wrap my food in aluminum foil in case you're wondering. But Evian also contained BPA. So, bisphenol A, which is an endocrine disruptor. That's a big problem because BPA was the whole reason that we switched from plastic water bottles, the Ngene bottles years ago, but Evian had a triple threat. It had BPA, it had uranium, and it had aluminum. So, even though they say it's made by the French Alps, it's natural electrolytes, it's natural spring water, it's pretty low quality, which isn't surprising. [music] As we've seen across these bottled waters, as the waters try and scale, the quality usually goes down. So, you've probably bought Evian in the last week or month. chunks.title: Conclusion and Recommendations
chunks.summary: In the conclusion, the speaker emphasizes the key takeaways about heavy metals and microplastics present in bottled waters. Recommendations for filtration methods and overall water quality awareness are provided, highlighting the importance of informed consumer choices.
chunks.transcript: pooh medal down here. We don't want this one. I would choose this one. What can you do? How can you fix this? Let's talk about solutions. So, if you guys want to see the full breakdown, all of the certificates of analysis that I promised you, you can go to my website. It's paulsaladinomd.com. I'll put a link in the description that goes directly to the water blog and the water results. So, here's what I want you guys to take away from this. Heavy metals were not a major problem in these waters. Some of them had trace amounts of arsenic, but in general, your biggest exposure to heavy metals are actually the foods you eat. potentially your tap water, which is a completely different story than bottled waters. But there's a way to fix that too, which I'll tell you about in one second. Metals are very common in things like seafood, chocolate, leafy greens, grains like rice, other grains. This is where you get the majority of your heavy metals. The majority of your heavy metals are probably not coming from bottled water. Microplastics are a different story. And again, the plastic bottled waters are the major issue here. 250,000 microplastics and nanoplastics when measured by a special set of microscopy at Columbia University in plastic water bottles. So don't be fooled when you hear that glass bottles have more microplastics than plastic bottle waters. That is bollocks. That is hogwash. That is baloney with a G. Don't worry about that. Worry about microplastics from bottled waters and plastic. That's your biggest problem. Also remember that some of these bottled waters like Evian had contamination with BPA or uranium like this aquapana. So you might want to avoid those long term. A lot of the systems today come with remmineralization salt or you can just add a pinch of sea salt to your water and that's going to put back most of the minerals that are lost in the process of reverse osmosis.
